### Part 1: General Solution for the Population Model

The differential equation provided is:
\[
\frac{dP}{dt} = rP \left(1 - \frac{P}{K}\right) + A \sin\left(\frac{2\pi t}{12}\right)P
\]

Substituting the given values \( r = 0.3 \), \( K = 1000 \), and \( A = 0.1 \):
\[
\frac{dP}{dt} = 0.3P \left(1 - \frac{P}{1000}\right) + 0.1 \sin\left(\frac{\pi t}{6}\right)P
\]

Factor out \( P \) on the right-hand side:
\[
\frac{dP}{dt} = P \left[ 0.3 \left(1 - \frac{P}{1000}\right) + 0.1 \sin\left(\frac{\pi t}{6}\right) \right]
\]
\[
\frac{dP}{dt} = P \left[ 0.3 - \frac{0.3P}{1000} + 0.1 \sin\left(\frac{\pi t}{6}\right) \right]
\]
\[
\frac{dP}{dt} = P \left[ \left(0.3 + 0.1 \sin\left(\frac{\pi t}{6}\right)\right) - \frac{0.3}{1000}P \right]
\]

This is a Bernoulli differential equation of the form \( P' + p(t)P = q(t)P^n \) (specifically with \( n=2 \)), or more directly, a logistic equation with a time-dependent growth rate. Let us define the time-dependent growth rate \( R(t) \) and the time-dependent carrying capacity \( K(t) \) conceptually, but to solve it analytically, we use the standard substitution for Bernoulli equations.
